text: The surgeon performs a pancreaticoduodenectomy using five laparoscopic ports arranged for upper abdominal surgery. The lesser sac is opened, exposing the inferior mesenteric vein below the pancreas. The superior mesenteric vein is visualized beneath the neck of the pancreas, with veins from the middle colic and pancreatic side converging into it. The hepatic flexure is aggressively mobilized, flipping the right colon to the left to expose the superior mesenteric portal vein axis and the uncinate process. The duodenum is cocarized, freeing it from the retroperitoneum and exposing the inferior vena cava and aorta up to the left border.

The surgeon removes pancreatic and peri-duodenal lymph nodes along with the specimen, peeling the root of the mesocolon away from the duodenum and uncinate process. Complete mobilization of the duodenum, retroperitoneum, and mesocolon is achieved, revealing the superior mesenteric portal vein axis. The inferior vena cava becomes visible, and mobilization continues from both sides of the inferior vena cava up to the left renal vein. Further peeling of the mesocolon from the retroperitoneum, duodenum, and uncinate process is performed, with lymph nodes identified for later removal.

The surgeon connects the superior mesenteric vein plane with the retroperitoneal plane, revealing tributaries from the pancreas to the superior mesenteric vein that require careful control to prevent bleeding. Lymph nodes in the para-aortic area are removed as part of the specimen. The junction between the two planes is visible, with remaining adhesions being gradually dissected. The duodenojejunal junction is opened, exposing the small bowel. Suprapancreatic dissection of lymph nodes in the common hepatic area is performed to expose the gastrointestinal artery and the suprapancreatic portal vein.

The surgeon visualizes the origin of the gastroduodenal artery and a segment of the portal vein between the hepatic artery and the pancreas. The gastroduodenal artery is cut between clips, revealing the portal vein more clearly. An instrument is passed from below the pancreas to the dissected area above, and a tape is inserted in the neck of the pancreas for traction. The surgical field includes the duodenum, head of the pancreas, uncinate process, superior mesenteric vein, and portal vein. The resection begins with cutting the stomach, pancreas, jejunum, and common bile duct. The pancreas is stapled with gentle, gradual compression to prevent parenchymal fracture.

The surgeon secures and ligates major venous tributaries, including the gastrocolic trunk of Henle, before continuing the separation of the vein from the uncinate process. The jejunum is transected and passed below the vessels to the right side, positioning the specimen for resection. The uncinate process is resected, followed by the cutting of the common bile duct. The superior mesenteric artery is clearly visible, and the surgeon meticulously dissects around the perineural plexus of the artery, ensuring the retroperitoneal margin of the uncinate process is in the proper plane for a negative margin.

The surgeon dissects upwards towards the common bile duct (CBD), aiming to suspend the specimen by the CBD and cut it as the final step. Using bipolar cautery and harmonic scalpel, the dissection progresses carefully along the surgical margin without encroaching on the uncinate process. An aberrant right hepatic artery is visible, originating from the superior mesenteric artery, passing behind the portal vein, and closely approaching the CBD. The CBD is cut, completing the resection, and the final view of the surgical field is shown.

The surgeon begins reconstruction by performing a hepatico-jejunostomy, inserting a gelatin catheter stent through the abdominal wall, jejunum, and into the hepatic ducts due to the small caliber of the common bile duct (CBD). A purse string suture is placed around the entry point, followed by a running suture using PDS-C-O to complete the anastomosis. The gastro-jejunostomy is then performed, with visible adequate margins and bulky lymph node areas. The ulcerated duodenum and opened head of the pancreas reveal the mass.
